question: \text { 1. For what integer } a \text { does } x^{2}-x+a \text { divide } x^{13}+x+90 \text { ? (page 577) }
solution:
Solution. Suppose \( x^{13}+x+90=\left(x^{2}-x+a\right) q(x) \) where \( a \) is an integer. Then \( q \) is a polynomial with integer coefficients. If \( a \leq 0 \), then \( x^{2}-x+a \), and hence also \( x^{13}+x+90 \), would have a non-negative zero, which is impossible. So \( a>0 \).

Substituting \( x=-1,0,1 \), we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
(a+2) q(-1) & =88, \\
a q(0) & =90, \\
a q(1) & =92 .
\end{aligned}
\]

The last two equations show that \( a \) divides 2 , so \( a=1 \) or 2 . But if \( a=1 \), then 3 would divide 88 . So \( a=2 \) is the only possibility and, in fact,
\[
\begin{aligned}
x^{13}+x+90= & \left(x^{2}-x+2\right)\left(x^{11}+x^{10}-x^{9}-3 x^{8}-x^{7}+5 x^{6}\right. \\
& \left.+7 x^{5}-3 x^{4}-7 x^{3}-11 x^{2}+23 x+45\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question: \text { 1. For what integer } parameter \text { does } variable^{2}-variable+parameter \text { divide } variable^{13}+variable+90 \text { ? (page 577) }
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( variable^{13}+variable+90=\left(variable^{2}-variable+parameter\right) quotient(variable) \) where \( parameter \) is an integer. Then \( quotient \) is a polynomial with integer coefficients. If \( parameter \leq 0 \), then \( variable^{2}-variable+parameter \), and hence also \( variable^{13}+variable+90 \), would have a non-negative zero, which is impossible. So \( parameter>0 \).

Substituting \( variable=-1,0,1 \), we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
(parameter+2) \, quotient(-1) & =88, \\
parameter \, quotient(0) & =90, \\
parameter \, quotient(1) & =92 .
\end{aligned}
\]

The last two equations show that \( parameter \) divides 2 , so \( parameter=1 \) or 2 . But if \( parameter=1 \), then 3 would divide 88 . So \( parameter=2 \) is the only possibility and, in fact,
\[
\begin{aligned}
variable^{13}+variable+90= & \left(variable^{2}-variable+2\right)\left(variable^{11}+variable^{10}-variable^{9}-3 variable^{8}-variable^{7}+5 variable^{6}\right. \\
& \left.+7 variable^{5}-3 variable^{4}-7 variable^{3}-11 variable^{2}+23 variable+45\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: \text { 1. For what integer } lighthouse \text { does } marigold^{2}-marigold+lighthouse \text { divide } marigold^{13}+marigold+90 \text { ? (page 577) }
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( marigold^{13}+marigold+90=\left(marigold^{2}-marigold+lighthouse\right) paintbrush(marigold) \) where \( lighthouse \) is an integer. Then \( paintbrush \) is a polynomial with integer coefficients. If \( lighthouse \leq 0 \), then \( marigold^{2}-marigold+lighthouse \), and hence also \( marigold^{13}+marigold+90 \), would have a non-negative zero, which is impossible. So \( lighthouse>0 \).

Substituting \( marigold=-1,0,1 \), we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
(lighthouse+2) paintbrush(-1) & =88, \\
lighthouse paintbrush(0) & =90, \\
lighthouse paintbrush(1) & =92 .
\end{aligned}
\]

The last two equations show that \( lighthouse \) divides 2 , so \( lighthouse=1 \) or 2 . But if \( lighthouse=1 \), then 3 would divide 88 . So \( lighthouse=2 \) is the only possibility and, in fact,
\[
\begin{aligned}
marigold^{13}+marigold+90= & \left(marigold^{2}-marigold+2\right)\left(marigold^{11}+marigold^{10}-marigold^{9}-3 marigold^{8}-marigold^{7}+5 marigold^{6}\right. \\
& \left.+7 marigold^{5}-3 marigold^{4}-7 marigold^{3}-11 marigold^{2}+23 marigold+45\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: \text { 1. For what integer } irrational \text { does } constantval^{2}-constantval+irrational \text { divide } constantval^{13}+constantval+90 \text { ? (page 577) }
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( constantval^{13}+constantval+90=\left(constantval^{2}-constantval+irrational\right) nonquotient(constantval) \) where \( irrational \) is an integer. Then \( nonquotient \) is a polynomial with integer coefficients. If \( irrational \leq 0 \), then \( constantval^{2}-constantval+irrational \), and hence also \( constantval^{13}+constantval+90 \), would have a non-negative zero, which is impossible. So \( irrational>0 \).

Substituting \( constantval=-1,0,1 \), we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
(irrational+2) nonquotient(-1) & =88, \\
irrational\, nonquotient(0) & =90, \\
irrational\, nonquotient(1) & =92 .
\end{aligned}
\]

The last two equations show that \( irrational \) divides 2 , so \( irrational=1 \) or 2 . But if \( irrational=1 \), then 3 would divide 88 . So \( irrational=2 \) is the only possibility and, in fact,
\[
\begin{aligned}
constantval^{13}+constantval+90= & \left(constantval^{2}-constantval+2\right)\left(constantval^{11}+constantval^{10}-constantval^{9}-3 constantval^{8}-constantval^{7}+5 constantval^{6}\right. \\
& \left.+7 constantval^{5}-3 constantval^{4}-7 constantval^{3}-11 constantval^{2}+23 constantval+45\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question: \text { 1. For what integer } zjrnksla \text { does } qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp+zjrnksla \text { divide } qzxwvtnp^{13}+qzxwvtnp+90 \text { ? (page 577) }
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Suppose \( qzxwvtnp^{13}+qzxwvtnp+90=\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp+zjrnksla\right) hbvcnqwe(qzxwvtnp) \) where \( zjrnksla \) is an integer. Then \( hbvcnqwe \) is a polynomial with integer coefficients. If \( zjrnksla \leq 0 \), then \( qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp+zjrnksla \), and hence also \( qzxwvtnp^{13}+qzxwvtnp+90 \), would have a non-negative zero, which is impossible. So \( zjrnksla>0 \).

Substituting \( qzxwvtnp=-1,0,1 \), we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
(zjrnksla+2) hbvcnqwe(-1) & =88, \\
zjrnksla hbvcnqwe(0) & =90, \\
zjrnksla hbvcnqwe(1) & =92 .
\end{aligned}
\]

The last two equations show that \( zjrnksla \) divides 2 , so \( zjrnksla=1 \) or 2 . But if \( zjrnksla=1 \), then 3 would divide 88 . So \( zjrnksla=2 \) is the only possibility and, in fact,
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp^{13}+qzxwvtnp+90= & \left(qzxwvtnp^{2}-qzxwvtnp+2\right)\left(qzxwvtnp^{11}+qzxwvtnp^{10}-qzxwvtnp^{9}-3 qzxwvtnp^{8}-qzxwvtnp^{7}+5 qzxwvtnp^{6}\right. \\
& \left.+7 qzxwvtnp^{5}-3 qzxwvtnp^{4}-7 qzxwvtnp^{3}-11 qzxwvtnp^{2}+23 qzxwvtnp+45\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
For which integer values of $a$ does the quadratic polynomial
\[
    x^{2}-x+a
\]
divide the polynomial
\[
    P(x)=x^{17}+x+100\ ?
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Suppose x^2-x+a divides P(x)=x^17+x+100 in \mathbb{Z}[x], so
   P(x)=(x^2-x+a)Q(x),  Q\in \mathbb{Z}[x].
1.  If a\leq 0 then \Delta =1-4a\geq 1 so x^2-x+a has a real root r\geq 1.  But P(r)=r^17+r+100>0, contradiction.  Hence a>0.
2.  Plug in x=0,1:  aQ(0)=P(0)=100,  aQ(1)=P(1)=1+1+100=102.  Thus a|100 and a|102 \Rightarrow  a|gcd(100,102)=2 \Rightarrow  a=1 or 2.
3.  (i)  If a=1 then x^2-x+1 has roots \omega =e^{\pm i\pi /3}, and \omega ^6=1 \Rightarrow  \omega ^17=\omega ^5.  Thus P(\omega )=\omega ^5+\omega +100=(\omega +\omega ^5)+100=1+100=101\neq 0, so no.
    (ii)  If a=2 then x^2-x+2 has roots \alpha  with \alpha ^2=\alpha -2.  One checks by the recurrence \alpha ^n=\alpha \cdot \alpha ^{n-1}-2\alpha ^{n-2} that \alpha ^17+\alpha +100=272\alpha -86\neq 0, so again no.
Therefore neither a=1 nor a=2 works, and no other integer is possible.  Conclusion: there is no integer a for which x^2-x+a divides x^17+x+100.

core_steps: Assume (x^2 − x + a) divides the given polynomial and let the quotient have integer coefficients.
Observe that a > 0; otherwise the quadratic factor would have a non-negative real root, contradicting the positive value of the dividend there.
Evaluate at x = 0 and x = 1 to get a q(0)=c and a q(1)=c+2, so a divides both c and c+2 ⇒ a | 2 ⇒ a ∈ {1,2}.
Evaluate at x = −1 to obtain (a+2) q(−1)=c−2; use this to rule out a=1, leaving a=2 as the only possibility.

slot1.description: Exponent of the highest-degree term in the dividend polynomial (currently 13 in x^13). It only needs to be odd so that (−1)^n = −1 in step 4.

slot2.description: Constant term of the dividend polynomial (currently 90). It can be replaced by any even integer not congruent to 2 mod 3, so that gcd(c, c+2)=2 and 3∤(c−2) in steps 3–4.
